title: Meta Senior Staff Eng (IC7) On Zuck Stories, Rapid Career Growth, Code Machine Archetype
guest_role: Ex-Meta Senior Staff Eng (IC7) by 27, #1 code committer at Meta, co-founder of Formation
key_takeaways: Novati joined Facebook as an intern in May 2009 (~200 engineers), skipping a planned HCI PhD at University of Washington; he was a 'code machine' from day one, merging two competing internal task tools in his first week and announcing it to the whole company.

His raw code output stayed roughly constant his whole career; what got him to IC7 by 27 was accumulated judgment and taste — knowing which parts of the codebase are sensitive, how changes affect other teams' in-flight migrations, and when speed is safe.

Signature IC7-scale wins were relentless solo migrations no team would have finished: manually refactoring away ~5-6,000 'Preparable' framework classes over months, and eliminating every legacy direct-hit PHP endpoint in favor of the routing framework.

Trust is the code machine's currency: after burning credibility with a rushed bug, he publicly told the push team 'if this breaks the site, I will resign' — seriously — to force his own triple-checking and rebuild trust with the teams that shipped his code.

As IC7 he spent ~30% of focus on his team and ~70% on codebase-wide initiatives, nominally reporting to junior managers while effectively reviewed by his skip and meeting a VP monthly; he aggressively declined meetings and standups to protect coding time.

Zuck stories: he built emoji-reactions-on-any-post with Zuckerberg at a 2009 hackathon (code too bad to ship, but the idea became the industry norm), and during the 2010 Google Plus lockdown a company-wide bet got Zuck to personally merge a PR in exchange for Novati giving up office ripsticking.

His popular internal newsletter (Weekly NTI) predicted a South San Francisco office, but he now calls it a mistake: off-the-cuff posts about second-class 'non-software-engineer' titles and which companies were poaching engineers forced executives into damage control — though Zuckerberg personally encouraged him to keep posting.

On LLMs: they are the Vim-to-VSCode leap repeated; he produces ~5x more code in Formation's ~500k-line codebase by prompting for code he already sees in his head. Near-term AI amplifies experienced engineers with strong taste; long-term, AI managing its own services behind API contracts may make human-readable code irrelevant.

Common traits in Meta's IC7+-only group regardless of archetype (code machine, specialist, fixer): extreme conscientiousness, attention to detail, and sharpness — groups moved through new technical proposals in minutes with rapid precise follow-ups.
His self-admitted weakness: he prioritized problems whose solutions he could already see in his head over the org's stated top priority, which he believes capped his impact and frustrated managers.

He left Meta after ~10 years when all-in mutual loyalty faded into a business relationship and vesting logistics meant an ~80% take-home pay drop; he estimates career growth was 50/50 luck (landing at 2009 Facebook) versus hard work, the only factor in your control.

Advice: stop treating feedback as a grade. The growth loop is: do something, get feedback from people whose taste you aspire to (not peers barely ahead of you), and genuinely action it — that cycle compounds exceptionally fast.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
notable_quotes.quote: More than once I've said this needs to get in and if it breaks the site, I will resign.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
notable_quotes.quote: That accumulation of judgment and taste is what crosses the line to the E7.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
notable_quotes.quote: Talent is something out of your control. And luck is outside of your control... hard work is the one thing that's within your control.

notable_quotes.speaker: Michael Novati
notable_quotes.quote: I was accepting feedback as a grade to judge myself and put pressure on myself to get a hundred percent next time, but I wasn't putting pressure on myself to actually improve.
